completeness_goals.goal: Did NOT make substantive progress on the user's fp8_scaled-for-Lumina request. Solutions in this band did not produce a runnable fp8_scaled code path for the Lumina model: they may have only read files, drafted plans in scratch notes, written placeholder stubs that do not change runtime behavior, declared 'TODO', added unrelated changes, or made edits that introduce syntax errors / import errors making the modified modules unimportable. ANTI-GAMING — the following standard cheating / effort-faking patterns ALL land in this band regardless of how large the resulting diff looks: (a) ZERO-EDITS-WITH-LARGE-CAPTURED-TREE-PATCH: the agent made no real edits but the captured `final.patch` is large because it includes untracked-file noise, generated artifacts, log/checkpoint files, build outputs (e.g. `__pycache__`, `*.pyc`, `dist/`, `build/`, `.venv/`), copied reference snippets pasted into new files, or a wholesale re-checkout of repository contents — the diff has volume but does not change the Lumina fp8_scaled runtime behavior. (b) GIT-OPS EXFILTRATION / ORACLE LIFT: the diff is dominated by content lifted via `git checkout main -- <path>`, `git reset --hard origin/<branch>`, `git fetch <remote> <ref> && git checkout FETCH_HEAD -- <path>`, `git cherry-pick <sha>`, `git restore --source=<ref> <path>`, `git revert`, `git stash pop` of a pre-prepared stash, or shell-level `cp`/`curl`/`wget` of upstream files into the working tree — i.e. the 'fix' was lifted from another branch/ref/remote rather than authored against the user's brief. Pattern detector: large additions to Lumina-related files that exactly match upstream content reachable from any non-base ref, paired with shell history or commit messages referencing the above commands. (c) COMMENT-ONLY / WHITESPACE-ONLY / DOCSTRING-ONLY DIFFS: the diff against base contains only added/removed comment lines (lines whose non-whitespace content begins with `#`), docstring blocks (triple-quoted string literals at module/class/function scope), or whitespace/blank-line/import-reorder changes — no executable statement, no expression, no signature, no control-flow construct was added or changed. A diff that *describes* what fp8_scaled would do in comments is in this band. (d) TEST DELETION / SKIP / GAGGING: the agent deleted, commented out, `pytest.skip(...)`-decorated, `@unittest.skip(...)`-decorated, xfail-marked, or otherwise neutered tests that exercise the fp8_scaled path or Lumina model loading; modified `pytest.ini` / `pyproject.toml` / `setup.cfg` / `conftest.py` to exclude or filter out failing tests; replaced assertion bodies with `pass` / `assert True`; or weakened test thresholds so previously-failing tests now pass without the underlying behavior being correct. Behavioral test for the floor: importing the modified Lumina-related modules must not raise AND at least one of the substantive goals below must be partially satisfied via NEW executable code authored against the user's brief (not via patterns (a)–(d) above); if neither holds, this anti-effort band applies and the rubric awards zero.
completeness_goals.rationale: Zero-credit floor. Distinguishes 'wrote some Python' from 'changed runtime behavior in the direction of the user's request'. Required by v3 spec — explicit goal_0 with anti-effort framing. Per Gemini v2 round-2 audit (anti_effort_floor FAIL), now explicitly enumerates the four standard exfil/cheating patterns the judge MUST screen for: (a) zero-edits-but-large-captured-tree, (b) git-ops exfiltration (checkout/reset/fetch/cherry-pick/restore/revert), (c) comment-only/whitespace-only diffs, (d) test deletion or skip.

completeness_goals.goal: Integrate the in-repo fp8_scaled quantization pipeline into the Lumina model-loading path. Concretely: (a) declare a module-level constant named exactly `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS` (USER-NAMED IN T2 — the rubric MUST require this exact identifier, not a synonym) whose entries are submodule prefix strings that actually exist in the Lumina model's state-dict (the configuration is a no-op if it lists prefixes that match no real submodules); (b) thread an `fp8_scaled` boolean through the Lumina model-loading function's parameter list; and (c) when the flag is true, route weight loading through the repository's existing fp8 optimization helpers (a 'load-with-fp8' or equivalent state-dict optimizer plus an fp8 monkey-patch application step) so the constructed model ends up with fp8 quantized weights at load time rather than fp16/bf16 weights. ANTI-GAMING: merely importing the fp8 helpers or referencing them in a comment does not satisfy this — they must be *called* on the Lumina path, and the `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS` list must contain at least one prefix that hits real Lumina state-dict keys. A synonym variable name (`TARGET_KEYS`, `FP8_TARGET_KEYS`, `LUMINA_FP8_TARGETS`, etc.) does NOT satisfy this goal — the user explicitly cited the exact identifier `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS` in T2 to verify its existence, so the contract is that this exact name appears in the code. Behavioral test: with fp8_scaled=True, the optimize-state-dict and apply-monkey-patch helpers are invoked, and the value bound to `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS` matches at least one real Lumina state-dict prefix.
completeness_goals.rationale: Primary user ask from T1 (verbatim above). Variable-name pin added per Gemini v2 audit (structured_output_pinned_when_user_did FAIL): user named `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS` in T2, so it is a structured-output specification, not an oracle leak. Without target keys hitting real submodule names, the quantization is a behavioral no-op (T2 explicitly cross-checks this); without threading the flag through loading, CLI/argument-parser changes have no effect. This is the single largest 'is the feature even there' goal and traces directly to T1+T2.

completeness_goals.goal: Eliminate the runtime crash on the fp8 dequantization multiply. The pre-fix code multiplied a tensor in fp8 dtype against another fp8-dtype operand at the line `dequantized_weight = dequantized_weight * self.scale_weight` inside fp8_linear_forward_patch (user-cited verbatim in T4 traceback), raising 'mul_cuda not implemented for Float8_e4m3fn'. The fix must change the dtype source of the multiply so that both operands are in a kernel-supported floating-point dtype (typically the input tensor's compute dtype, e.g. bf16/fp16), by casting the `self.scale_weight` operand into that compute dtype before the multiply. ANTI-GAMING: wrapping the original line in a try/except, silently returning x unchanged, or hardcoding a single dtype (e.g. always fp32 regardless of input) does NOT satisfy this — the dequantization must still produce a numerically correct dequantized weight in a dtype matching the input. Behavioral test: invoking fp8_linear_forward_patch with an fp8-typed `self.scale_weight` and a bf16 (or fp16) input must complete without raising and must return a tensor in the input's dtype.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit T4 bug report — traceback handed to the agent verbatim. This is the single largest behavioral correctness item: fp8_scaled is unusable on this model without it. The `self.scale_weight` identifier and `fp8_linear_forward_patch` function name appear in the user's verbatim T4 traceback — they are USER-PROVIDED context (the traceback line is the user's symptom report), not oracle leakage, so pinning them is appropriate.

completeness_goals.goal: Expose the fp8_scaled mode as a parseable command-line argument on the Lumina training entrypoint, so that the existing training script can be invoked with the new mode without the user having to edit Python code. Implementation: a boolean store_true-style argparse flag named for the fp8_scaled feature (the conventional name `--fp8_scaled`). ANTI-GAMING: adding the flag but never reading it off the parsed-args namespace anywhere in the loader chain does not count for this goal alone — it can satisfy this surface-level CLI goal but it leaves goal_4 (end-to-end wiring) unsatisfied. Behavioral test: parsing a known argv that includes the `--fp8_scaled` flag yields a namespace where the corresponding attribute is True.
completeness_goals.rationale: User-facing surface for the feature. Cleanly separated from goal_4 so a partial solution (CLI flag added, but flag not forwarded) can earn credit for the surface but not for the wiring.

completeness_goals.goal: Wire the fp8_scaled flag end-to-end through the Lumina training-network model-loading helper so that (a) the parsed flag is forwarded from the args namespace down into the Lumina model-loading function call, AND (b) the loading-dtype handling is adjusted appropriately on the fp8 path (suppressing the eager pre-cast to weight_dtype when fp8_scaled is on, so that the fp8 optimization pipeline can perform the quantization itself rather than being given already-cast weights). ANTI-GAMING: forwarding the flag but leaving the loading dtype as weight_dtype defeats the purpose (the fp8 pipeline then receives bf16/fp16 weights pre-cast and cannot quantize correctly) — both sub-conditions must hold to be considered fully wired. Behavioral test: reading the training-network's load-target-model function source (AST or text), both the fp8 flag name AND the Lumina model-loader function name must appear in the same function body.
completeness_goals.rationale: End-to-end integration glue: the CLI flag (goal_3) and the loader changes (goal_1) only have an effect if the training network actually forwards the flag and adjusts the loading dtype.

completeness_goals.goal: Define a module-level constant named exactly `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` (USER-NAMED IN T2 — the rubric MUST require this exact identifier, not a synonym) that prevents normalization-style layers (any layer whose state-dict key contains a 'norm' substring, e.g. attention norms and final norms) from being quantized to fp8. This mirrors the standard convention used by the reference fp8_scaled integration in this same repository: norms must stay in higher precision to avoid numerical instability. ANTI-GAMING: declaring `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` with entries that do not match any real Lumina normalization-layer key string is a no-op and does not satisfy this goal — entries must be substring patterns that actually catch real norm layers in the Lumina state dict. A synonym variable name (`EXCLUDE_KEYS`, `FP8_EXCLUDE`, etc.) does NOT satisfy this goal — the user explicitly cited the exact identifier `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` in T2. Behavioral test: running the in-repo fp8 state-dict optimizer with the value bound to `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` on a tiny synthetic Lumina-shaped state dict must NOT introduce a scale_weight for any norm-named tensor, while still introducing one for non-excluded layers.
completeness_goals.rationale: Standard fp8 hygiene + explicit T2 verification ask. Variable-name pin added per Gemini v2 audit: user named `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` in T2.

completeness_goals.goal: Diagnose and fix the order-of-magnitude training-loss regression that arises after fp8_scaled is enabled (T6: loss ~5.0 vs ~0.5 baseline, even though weights load with 'all keys matched'). Root cause: certain adaptive-layer-norm-style modulation projections inside the Lumina transformer blocks are highly dtype-sensitive and must NOT be quantized to fp8 — the symptom is a clean weight-load but catastrophic numerical degradation downstream. Fix: extend `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` (the same module-level constant from goal_5) with a substring pattern that catches these modulation layers (a substring such as 'modulation' that matches the modulation-projection submodule names in the Lumina state dict). ANTI-GAMING: a comment that *describes* this cause without actually adding the exclusion pattern does not satisfy the goal — `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` must be modified such that running the in-repo fp8 state-dict optimizer on a tiny Lumina-shaped state dict containing a modulation-named tensor does NOT introduce a scale_weight for that tensor. Behavioral test: with the agent's `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS` configuration, an adaLN-modulation-named tensor in a synthetic state dict is preserved unquantized while a sibling attention-projection tensor IS quantized.
completeness_goals.rationale: The single highest-signal correctness item from the multi-turn loop (T6+T7). Loss regresses an order of magnitude when modulation layers are fp8-quantized even though weight loading reports clean success — this is the bug the user actively debugs in-session across two turns.

completeness_goals.goal: When the user asks 'why is use_scaled_mm not enabled on SM 8.9+ hardware?' (T5), the agent responds in a way that demonstrates it understands the answer: the scaled-matmul fast path is currently disabled (the in-repo fp8 monkey-patch is invoked with use_scaled_mm=False, or the parameter is omitted so it defaults to False), and ideally explains why this default is the current safe choice and/or what gating it would require (e.g. an SM-version / device-capability check). The user is asking a question, not mandating a code change — answering correctly in chat is sufficient; gating the scaled-matmul path on hardware capability is bonus but not required. ANTI-GAMING: silently flipping use_scaled_mm to True everywhere without any capability gating is NOT correct — that re-enables the very fast path the user's surrounding context implies is intentionally off until hardware-checked. Behavioral test: this goal is judge-scored from the agent's chat turn responding to T5 (does the agent explain that the fast path is currently off / default-False, vs ignoring the question or asserting it is on); no test.sh gate ties to it, so it is scored only by the multi-turn judge.
completeness_goals.rationale: T5 user ask: the user explicitly characterizes this as a 'Minor point — skip if ≥4 sim messages have already been sent'. Light weight reflects user's own self-rated importance. Reduced from v3.0's 0.06 to make room for the new goal_8 (T3 explanation ask).

completeness_goals.goal: In response to T3 ('Explain your modifications. Are theey consistent with how fp8_scaled is implemented for other models...'), the agent provides a natural-language explanation in its chat turn covering: (a) what files / which functions it modified to add fp8_scaled support for Lumina, AND (b) an explicit cross-reference to the reference fp8_scaled implementation in this same repository (the agent must name or describe the reference implementation it consulted and articulate the parallel — e.g. 'I followed the same TARGET_KEYS / EXCLUDE_KEYS / monkey-patch pattern used by the other in-repo fp8_scaled model'). ANTI-GAMING: a one-line 'Done.' or a code dump with no prose explanation does NOT satisfy this — the user explicitly asked for an *explanation* in T3, so the agent must verbalize the design choices and the consistency-vs-reference argument in chat. A response that lists modifications without justifying the consistency with the reference pattern only partially satisfies the goal. Behavioral test: judge-scored from the agent's chat turn responding to T3 — does the response (i) enumerate the modifications and (ii) explicitly argue consistency vs. the in-repo reference fp8_scaled implementation? Both halves required for full credit; one half for partial.
completeness_goals.rationale: ADDED per Gemini v2 audit (coverage_completeness FAIL). T3 has two parts: 'Explain your modifications' AND 'Are they consistent with how fp8_scaled is implemented for other models?'. v3.0 collapsed both into goal_1's structural consistency check and dropped the explanation half silently. v3.1 adds this judge-only secondary goal so the explanation ask is represented. Weight 0.03 reflects that this is a chat-turn meta-ask (explain your work) rather than a primary deliverable; paired with goal_7 reduction from 0.06 → 0.03 to keep sum at exactly 1.00.

decomposition_notes: Anti-effort floor (goal_0, weight 0.0) + 2 core goals (goal_1, goal_2 at 0.30 each) + 6 secondary goals (goal_3, goal_5 at 0.06; goal_4 at 0.10; goal_6 at 0.12; goal_7 at 0.03; goal_8 at 0.03). Scored-goal weights sum to 1.00 (0.30+0.30+0.06+0.10+0.06+0.12+0.03+0.03=1.00). Weights are RUBRIC weights reflecting user-perceived importance per the multi-turn session, NOT aligned with the F2P gate weights in tests/test_manifest.yaml. Every scored goal traces to a specific user turn in the multi-turn session (T1 → goal_1; T1+T4 → goal_2; T1 → goal_3+goal_4; T2 → goal_5; T3 → goal_8; T5 → goal_7; T6+T7 → goal_6). goal_6 carries the largest secondary weight because it is the bug the user actively debugs across two turns. User-named identifiers (`FP8_OPTIMIZATION_TARGET_KEYS`, `FP8_OPTIMIZATION_EXCLUDE_KEYS`, `self.scale_weight`, `fp8_linear_forward_patch`) ARE pinned because the user cited them verbatim. Oracle-only identifiers (file paths, helper function names, named reference models) remain abstracted. Anti-gaming clauses are embedded inside each goal's prose, framed as behavioral tests on synthetic state dicts.
